skill.name: bash-linux
skill.description: Bash/Linux terminal patterns. Critical commands, piping, error handling, scripting. Use when working on macOS or Linux systems.
skill.summary: Essential bash patterns for terminal workflows, file operations, process management, and scripting
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static scanner detected 92 patterns flagged as potential security issues. After manual review, all findings are confirmed FALSE POSITIVES. The skill file is educational documentation containing bash command examples in markdown code blocks and tables. Backticks and command syntax are part of markdown formatting for documentation purposes, not executable code. No malicious intent detected, no security risks identified.

content.user_title: Master Bash and Linux Terminal Commands
content.value_statement: Generate correct bash commands and scripts without memorization. Claude Code provides accurate patterns for file operations, process management, text processing, and error handling in Linux and macOS environments.
actual_capabilities: Generate accurate bash command syntax for file operations like search, find, and text processing
Create bash scripts with proper error handling using set -euo pipefail and trap patterns
Provide correct command chaining with operators like &&, ||, and | for workflow automation
Explain differences between bash and PowerShell for users transitioning between systems
Suggest appropriate text processing tools (grep, sed, awk, cut, sort) for specific data manipulation tasks
Debug common bash scripting issues with environment variables and shell scripting patterns
limitations: Does not execute commands directly - provides command patterns for you to run in your terminal
Examples focus on common POSIX-compliant tools - may not cover all Linux distribution variations
Advanced system administration tasks like systemd service configuration require additional domain knowledge
Windows-specific bash environments (WSL, Git Bash) may have subtle differences from native Linux

use_cases.title: Developer workflow automation
use_cases.description: Software developers use this skill to automate repetitive tasks like file batch processing, log monitoring, and build pipelines. Reduces time spent searching for correct command syntax.
use_cases.target_user: Software developer working on Unix-based systems

use_cases.title: System administration scripting
use_cases.description: DevOps engineers and sysadmins reference these patterns to write reliable bash scripts with proper error handling. Helps create maintainable automation scripts for server management.

use_cases.title: Learning bash fundamentals
use_cases.description: New users transitioning from Windows to Linux or macOS learn correct command patterns and understand bash scripting best practices. Provides quick reference without searching multiple documentation sources.
use_cases.target_user: Developer new to Linux or macOS environments

prompt_templates.title: Basic file search command
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I find all JavaScript files modified in the last 7 days?
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to locate specific files in a codebase

prompt_templates.title: Script with error handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a bash script template that handles errors properly and includes cleanup on exit
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a reliable script template for automation tasks

prompt_templates.title: Text processing pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I extract the second column from a CSV file and count unique values?
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to analyze log files or data exports

output_examples.input: How do I find all Python files and count them?
output: To find all Python files recursively:

output_examples.input: Show me a script that backs up a directory
output: Here's a backup script with error handling:

output_examples.input: How do I check if a command succeeded?
output: You can check exit status with $?:

best_practices: Always quote variables to prevent word splitting and glob expansion: use "$VAR" instead of $VAR
Use set -euo pipefail at the start of scripts to catch errors early and fail on undefined variables
Test destructive commands with echo first: replace rm with echo rm to see what would be deleted
Prefer [[ over [ for conditionals as it's safer and more portable in modern bash scripts
anti_patterns: Don't parse ls output - use find or glob patterns instead as ls output is not designed for parsing
Avoid eval on user input - it allows arbitrary code execution and is a major security vulnerability
Don't use cd in scripts without error checking - use absolute paths or subshells (cd ... || exit)
Avoid looping over ls output - use shell globbing (for file in *.txt) instead for reliable file iteration

faq.question: Why does my bash script fail when I copy commands directly?
faq.answer: Scripts run in a non-interactive shell without your aliases and PATH customizations. Always use full paths and test scripts with bash -x scriptname.sh to debug.

faq.question: What's the difference between single quotes and double quotes?
faq.answer: Double quotes allow variable expansion and command substitution: "$VAR" expands to the value. Single quotes treat everything literally: '$VAR' stays as the literal string.

faq.question: How do I handle spaces in filenames?
faq.answer: Always quote variables: use "$file" instead of $file. Use while IFS= read -r line for reading files, and find -print0 with xargs -0 for null-separated handling.

faq.question: Why does my script say 'command not found'?
faq.answer: The command isn't in your PATH. Use the full path (/usr/bin/command) or add the directory to PATH: export PATH="$PATH:/new/path". Use command -v cmdname to check if a command exists.

faq.question: What's the difference between && and ;?
faq.answer: The && operator runs the second command only if the first succeeds (exit code 0). The semicolon runs both commands regardless, continuing even if the first fails.

faq.question: How do I debug a bash script?
faq.answer: Run with bash -x script.sh to print each command before execution. Add set -x in your script to enable debugging for specific sections. Use set -v to print lines as they're read.
